If you're looking for a tactical shooter that respects your time, your skill, or your wallet, Delta Force isn't it. What could have been a solid addition to the genre has quickly spiraled into a playground for hackers and a masterclass in poor game design. It’s free-to-play, and frankly, that’s the only reason anyone is still clicking "Launch."  The Cheating Epidemic The biggest nail in the coffin is the rampant hacking. The game is currently infested with players using aimbots and map hacks. There is zero competitive integrity when you’re being sniped from across the map by someone who clearly has a "perfect" line of sight through solid walls. Until the developers implement a serious, aggressive anti-cheat, the matches are essentially meaningless.  Broken Gunplay & Hit Registration Even when you aren't fighting a bot, you’re fighting the game’s terrible hit registration. There is nothing more infuriating than getting the drop on an enemy, lining up a clean headshot from behind, and unloading—only for the game to barely acknowledge the hits. The "hit markers" feel like a suggestion rather than a fact. You can dump half a mag into someone's skull, only for them to do a 180-degree turn and one-shot kill you with zero effort. The TTK (Time to Kill) feels inconsistent and completely weighted against the player.  The Spawn-Death Loop The spawn system is a disaster. Unlike almost every other modern FPS, Delta Force seems to have forgotten the concept of spawn immunity. You will frequently find yourself dying five times in a row simply spawning. You spawn, you die, you repeat. It’s a waste of patience and ruins any sense of flow or strategy.  Final Verdict It’s easy to see why the reviews have plummeted to "Mixed." This is a "trash" experience that feels like a low-effort cash grab disguised as a tactical shooter. Do not spend a dime on in-game purchases. Do not invest your time expecting a fair fight. Unless the developers prioritize fixing the hacking problem and overhaul the hit detection, this game is a complete waste of money and sanity. Save your hard drive space.

Stardew Valley is such a fun and relaxing game. It’s the kind of game you can play for hours without even realizing it. Whether you’re farming, fishing, mining, or just talking to the townspeople, there’s always something to do.  I really like how you can play at your own pace and make your farm however you want. It never feels rushed or stressful, which makes it super enjoyable. The characters are also really charming and make the town feel alive.  Overall, it’s a simple but super addictive game that’s perfect if you just want to chill and have a good time.

One of my all time favorite games. I've bought it on 3 different platforms, including Steam, and have logged over 300 hours. This game is a classic in farm sims for a reason. Once you get used to the time restrictions, it's addictive, and it feels very rewarding to see your farm flourish. This is my comfort game on bad days.  Sebastian will always be my husband, no matter how much I try to choose someone else on a different playthrough. I just can't do it. The silly emo boi won my heart. Together we have kids, a cat, a frog named Toad, a toad named Frog, and the weirdest little farm because I can't do aesthetic layouts to save my life. I'll feed my emo husband as much sashimi and pumpkin soup as his little pixel heart can handle, and we'll live happily in every playthrough until the farm fields are razed by time.  10/10, would highly recommend Stardew Valley.

This game was awesome when it launched.  Back then I was capable of following the chaos, but in the ensuing seasons it has just become... too much.  On top of it being miserable to play vanguards/tanks as you legitimately are mocked by the game with less competitive points for the same (if often more due to solo tanking) efforts, there are so many new characters who are teleporting and jumping wildly and are ridiculously difficult to track.  Then you have, on the regular, level 100+ maxed out Spidermans who not only force triple DPS in comp matches, but then earn 5 kills over the course of two rounds of convoy.  No swaps, no care, just Main Protagonist Syndrome ruining everyone else's experience in a team game.  The selfishness and maliciousness in Rivals permeates through everything, and it really feels like an intentional design pillar by NetEase at this point.  It is wild watching this game go through the same mistakes and pains as Overwatch did, though even with role queue in the latter the matchmaking is just baffling in both games.  Wild and frustrating.

Do not let the number of hours fool you. I've been playing this since it was pre beta and across multiple platforms (PC, Switch, PS4, Xbox One) over the years. It is one of those games I've been coming back to over and over with new content, updates, and tinkering of various classes as time goes on, Everything is available on all platforms save for some skins that are locked to the platform they are purchased on.  There's a lot going on in this game. Different factions, planets, mission types, and currency. On currency there is only two types you can pay for with one being able to be traaded for amongst players and bought while the other is soley used in one specific part Prime Warframes and other things related to them. All the other ones are earned through gameplay and other activities.  The game is also very grindy and there are time locks for building items that range from a minute to three days for builfing the warframes themselves regardless of if they are prime or not. Other than that most the levels have a bit of randomness to them. Layouts are randomized to an extent but you can pick up on how they are after awhile. This helps with how repetitive things can get and are.  The frames themselves are spread over like 60ish frames now from the three we used to have. Some might seem repetitive but each brings their own unique flavor and playstyle. The intial three are a speed based frame that foes fast with a shield, one that can strip shields from enemies, fling back rounds fired at her, and lift then crush enemies, the last is a swordsman that can move fast and swing faster. I'll let you figure out which one is my favorite.  The weapons are also as varied as them spread over three types (Melee, Primary, and Secondary), These range from basic pistols, rifles, shotguns to more exotic things such as whips, energy weapons, and things that are infested. Don't worry the infested stuff makes sense after you get to a point but the short of it is that there's a virus that can infect living and non living thing.  In addition to the weapon types theres also damage types (piercing, blunt, and slashing) with base elemental stuff that can also be combined on the weapon to make MORE types of damage to play with.  With all this being said don't be afraid to look at a guide. I actually recomend it so you dont get softlocked somewhere without realizing it. There's lots to do and the game isn't very foward with things at times though it has gotten much better for the new player missions.  Don't be afraid to ask questions in chat and join a guild.  Do your star chart and hope this has helped!

Highly recommend giving this a try if you're looking for an alternative BR/Combat focused PvP game.  The game runs smoothly without any hiccups & looks visually pleasing. I've encountered zero issues with regards to frame drops, stuttering or crashing, pretty much works straight out of the box on Linux which is refreshing.  The gameplay & combat itself feels highly rewarding however, the learning curve is steep & I don't think there is an actual skill ceiling to this game or if there is, I can't see it yet...theres a lot of mechanics to be mastered.  Ranked gameplay is locked behind hitting level 50 (takes considerable time) or level 20 & upgrading to a 'paid' edition of the game which is very reasonably priced at approximately £6/$9.  There is plenty of ongoing events available for users to earn free cosmetics & the cash shop is aggressively priced to entice engagement. As far as I'm aware, everything in the cash shop is cosmetic only and does not provide any advantage in game.  I'm highly impressed with Naraka so far & will hopefully continue to enjoy what it has to offer.

This free to play visual novel with adventure and roleplaying game (RPG) elements by NLB project features two short stories in Am I a Monster and Return of Red Riding Hood, New Adventures. In Am I a Monster, it features an unnamed male protagonist who has suddenly turned into a wolf and is hunted by fearful townspeople and police officers. The protagonist (the player) must decide what to kill and to eat as he gradually becomes more attuned as a wolf or retains his human form.  In Return of Red Riding Hood, New Adventures, it continues with a parody of the classic children's fairy tale where the mother of the same unnamed girl in NLB project's earlier game Return of Red Riding Hood Enhanced Edition tries to tell a bedtime story of Little Red Riding Hood to her daughter to get her to sleep at night.  As a young teenage girl Little Red Riding Hood, she lives with her mother and receives a task by her mother to gather ingredients to make a basket of sorrel, ham or cottage cheese pastries to deliver to her grandmother who is a forester and lives alone in a cottage inside a forest. After making the pastries, the game will transition to the visual novel part of the game where Red Riding Hood or her mother will deliver the pastries to their grandmother and encounter Wolf and/or woodcutters along the way.  Having played this game for over an hour to get all endings and achievements, the RPG elements in Am I a Monster is a highlight of this short story where the ending and a few encounters will turn out differently depending on whether the protagonist becomes more attuned as a wolf or not.  In Return of Red Riding Hood, New Adventures, the funny and imaginative scriptwriting in this short story is the main highlight of this entire free to play game by itself. It retains the same amount of comedic humour from Return of Red Riding Hood Enhanced Edition that can be ridiculous and funny. Having played the earlier game Return of Red Riding Hood Enhanced Edition, there are less tragic endings this time, possibly because the mother knows better how to get the girl to sleep without making her cry, unlike the girl's brother. As such, I have also decided to shorten the Big Bad Wolf to just Wolf as there are fewer bad endings involving Wolf actually devouring Little Red Riding Hood or any member of her family.  All the endings and achievements are easy to obtain where a published community guide is available at the time of this review. Hope this game will have a higher confidence metric in future.  Among the cons, in Am I a Monster, it feels too short and doesn't give enough variety in the form of having more differences between being more attuned as a wolf or as a human being. This leads to a bit of wasted potential as an RPG which could possibly keep readers interested in the game longer. When comparing the number of endings in Am I a Monster with Return of Red Riding Hood, New Adventures, the former has much fewer plot branches and endings. The plot and storyline is still interesting enough to read through in any case. In Return of Red Riding Hood, New Adventures, due to the large number of plot branches and endings, the short story only has 5 save slots available, it would be nice to have more save slots for this game or short story.  Recommended as a free to play game with nice artwork, classical background music and for those who like a short visual novel featuring wolves in two short stories, one of which contains many comedic twists to the classic fairy tale.  ░░██▄░░░░░░░░░░▄██░░ ░▄▀░█▄░░░░░░░░▄█░░█░ ░█░▄░█▄░░░░░░▄█░▄░█░ ░█░██████████████▄█░ ░█████▀▀████▀▀█████░ ▄█▀█▀░░░████░░░▀▀███ ██░░▀████▀▀████▀░░██ ██░░░░█▀░░░░▀█░░░░██ ███▄░░░░░░░░░░░░▄███ ░▀███▄░░████░░▄███▀░ ░░░▀██▄░▀██▀░▄██▀░░░ ░░░░░░▀██████▀░░░░░░ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  Visual novel, JRPG, Anime Steam group - Join the group and consider following the group's curator list for more reviews, free to play games or comedic visual novels.

Let me paint you a picture: you and your buddy spend three hours meticulously gearing up. You've hit every military base, dodged zombies, carefully managed your hunger and thirst, and finally have decent weapons, medical supplies, and enough ammo to feel confident. You're ready to take on the world.  Then you get teleported into the sky and fall to your death. Or some guy with an AK that never needs to reload headshots you both from 800 meters away. Or my personal favorite – the invisible cheater who punches you unconscious while you're looting a building.  This isn't a rare occurrence in DayZ; it's the standard gameplay loop. The developers seem to have completely abandoned any pretense of anti-cheat measures. Why bother building a survival game when you can just let script kiddies ruin everyone's day?  The base game itself is frustrating enough – clunky controls, bugs that will make you want to punch your monitor, and a community that makes the worst of Rust look like a knitting circle. But the cheating? It's so pervasive that it's not even an "if" you'll encounter them, but "when" and "how embarrassingly."  My friend and I died to the same teleporting cheater three times in one day. Each time, we'd respawn, spend another hour gearing up, only to be instantly wiped out by some kid with mommy's credit card and a god complex.  Save your money. Save your sanity. Go play literally anything else. This game has been in development for a decade and still plays like an early access alpha from 2015 – except with more cheaters than players.  0/10, would rather slam my fingers in a car door than subject myself to this again.  Have you encountered similar issues with cheaters in DayZ, or was this just our particularly unlucky experience?

[b]I recommend Dead by Daylight — but I need to start with a serious warning.[/b]  The community in this game can be extremely toxic, and it’s not something the developers have managed to address effectively so far. If you’re not in a good place mentally, I would strongly suggest to think twice before getting into this game. Your self-worth will be tested, and negative interactions are common. Within my first 100 hours, I was already told to take my own life because people didn't like how I played.  That’s not about the horror setting or the violence in the game itself — it’s about the players, and the lack of effective systems to deal with this behavior. Because of that alone, I would not recommend this game to younger players or anyone without some level of emotional resilience.  [hr][/hr]  [h2]Why I Still Recommend It[/h2]  With that said, the gameplay itself is what kept me playing.  Dead by Daylight has a very unique and engaging core loop: one killer versus a team of survivors. Survivors try to waste the killer’s time and complete objectives, while the killer applies pressure and hunts them down. It’s simple on the surface, but it has become very complex over the years.  There are hundreds of perks, and each character brings their own options. You build loadouts with combinations of perks, which allows for a lot of variety — even if there is a clear meta and you’ll see certain perks very often.  [hr][/hr]  [h2]Learning Curve & Content[/h2]  The learning curve is steep. There’s a huge amount of knowledge to pick up, and you’ll likely spend time outside the game watching guides or learning mechanics. Personally, I enjoyed that process.  The game and its DLC can get expensive, but you are also getting a large amount of content in return.  [hr][/hr]  [h2]Final Verdict[/h2]  [b]I recommend Dead by Daylight — but with that initial warning in mind.[/b]  If you can handle the community and push through the learning curve, there is a unique and rewarding game underneath.

Overall opinion- I know there is a lot of hate towards this game and there's some pretty good reason for it; moderation is... "suboptimal", the vast majority of the player base learned how to walk a month ago, and there's a ton of trolling that happens. But, despite the numerous downsides this game has, I still recommend it, there's plenty of good people to meet here. If you find the right group of people to hang out with, you will have a great time and want to come back frequently.   People- As you've probably heard, there's a lot of strange things that happen in certain worlds, but I've only run into it in public lobbies once or twice in the long time I've played the game. It's pretty avoidable. The main times you'll run into it is if you are intentionally looking for it. A big issue I've had with the player base is that they are way to young to be on a game like this. Just judging by their voices, there is no way most of them are older than 9. But there are plenty of people that are friend material, no matter what kind of person you are. I've met plenty of people on this game that I will never forget, for good reason. I've never had a game that has affected me like this one, the social experiences you can have is honestly like no other.   Worlds- If you look around, you can find a lot of strange worlds. Whether it be the name, focus, or group it targets; there is no shortage of very odd worlds. If you want to explore them, have at it, there's some that will give you a good laugh. But there's some worlds that are amazing and most of them are severely under-rated. There's worlds that look stunningly beautiful, especially it being in VR, it can be stunning. There's worlds that are very entertaining, my personal favorite being VRC Drum Hero. There's also some social instances that can be fun, but me personally (not being the social type of person) haven't explored them a lot. One of my favorite things to do is just explore worlds with friends, it's super fun and would totally recommend you try it.   Advice- -If you want a lobby that is moderated, just join group instances. -If you want to avoid little kids as much as possible, join group instances that are focused at popular subjects kids like, most notably roblox. Other than that, the experience you get out of the game is completely for you to make.

Very good game for socializing and hanging out! This is essentially what the Metaverse and ROBLOX wishes they were. Very community-oriented with a TON of freedom given to the players, with comfort settings for just about anyone. Even has built-in streaming and camera tools to make it easy to make your own machinimas and whatnot!  This game is especially awesome on the monetary side. Pretty much everything is free, but you can choose to pay for avatars and additional features. It runs on a similar model as WinZIP and Photopea; people will inevitably pay for free things that they genuinely feel are worth their time and support, and this tends to be one of those :']  A few things worth noting: 1. Lots of weirdoes, but that's nothing new to social games. Overall, lobbies will either be entirely chill (and will collectively shun any weirdoes who join), or they'll have one or two weirdoes that ruin the entire thing. And "weirdo" really just means gooners/perverts, racists, and trolls in this scenario. Just mute those people and move on, usually that works! 2. Performance can be a nightmare in VR due to all the unoptimized user-generated content, ESPECIALLY on AMD graphics. I would recommend downgrading your AMD drivers to play this game; the exact version depends on your model, and I'd advise joining the Virtual Reality Discord server to inquire further!  Side note, if you do plan to get a PCVR headset, the Oculus Quest 3 still remains the most bang-for-your-buck option, although I'd definitely recommend getting a halo headstrap of some kind alongside it.  That's all! Very good game! Highly recommend!

Bro, this game is basically a chill trucking sim that slowly takes over your life 🚛💨  Euro Truck Simulator starts off like: “I’ll just do one quick delivery” and next thing you know it’s 3AM and you’re locked in, hauling cargo across half of Europe.  At some point you realize… you’re not just playing anymore. You’re actually in truck mode.  Gameplay?  Drive ✔️ Indicators ✔️ Thinking about life at 2AM on a dark highway ✔️  No action, no stress. Just you, your truck, and a long road where everything somehow feels calm.  You begin like:  “this is kinda funny ngl”  And end like:  “I need better routes, better fuel economy, and more drivers.”  And trust me, you WILL:  start following speed limits like it matters feel wrong skipping sleep stops turn on music and just cruise get annoyed at traffic like it’s real life 💀  Graphics? Nothing insane, but night driving in the rain? that hits different.  It’s giving:  “lowkey therapy” “work, but peaceful” “Fast & Furious if it was realistic”  And somehow… delivering random cargo becomes your whole purpose.  Final verdict: 10/10 would definitely lose sleep driving across Europe again 🚛✨

I've sunk a stupid amount of hours into Euro Truck Simulator 2, and honestly, it’s one of those games that just keeps pulling you back in. On the surface it sounds simple, drive trucks across Europe, but once you get into it, it becomes weirdly addictive.  What makes ETS2 amazing is the freedom. You can just chill, throw on some music, and cruise through different countries, or you can go full management mode and build up your own trucking company with garages, drivers, and custom trucks. The map is massive, and the attention to detail in cities, roads, and scenery makes it feel surprisingly immersive.  One of the best parts, though, is the multiplayer scene. Using TruckersMP completely changes the experience. Suddenly the roads feel alive, you’re passing real players, forming convoys, and sometimes getting stuck in hilarious traffic jams at popular routes. It makes the world feel way more dynamic than singleplayer alone.  The modding support is also huge. There are tons of mods out there, new trucks, maps, graphics upgrades, realistic physics, you can tweak the game exactly how you want it. It keeps the game fresh even after hundreds of hours.  Overall, ETS2 is one of those rare games that can be relaxing, immersive, and social all at the same time. Whether you want to chill after a long day or drive in massive multiplayer convoys, there’s always something to do. If you enjoy simulation games or just want something laid-back but engaging, ETS2 is absolutely worth playing.

Season 2 turned this game into a system crash simulator.  About 10–15 minutes in, my entire PC hard-freezes. Not a crash, not an error—just a full lock that forces a hard power reset. That’s the kind of issue you expect from unstable overclocks or faulty hardware… except my system is completely stable everywhere else.  Before this update? No problems. After this update? Unplayable.  Specs for context: RX 7900 XTX Nitro+ Vapor-X Ryzen 7 7800X3D 32GB DDR5 6000MHz  Every other game runs perfectly fine. Only this one manages to freeze my entire system.  And the “fix”? People suggesting BIOS tweaks like enabling the integrated GPU. Seriously? I’m supposed to start changing motherboard-level settings just to keep a AAA game from locking up my PC?  That’s not a workaround—that’s an indictment.  Pushing an update that can hard-freeze systems like this is unacceptable. This isn’t a minor bug, it’s a fundamental stability failure.  At this point I’m not even comfortable launching the game anymore.  Fix it.

If you're a BF fan you will find enjoyment somewhere between BFV and BF3. If you are not a BF fan and new to the franchise, you'll be blown away or hate it. As someone who isn't particularly anal about features in a game, this is probably the 3rd best BF game I've played personally. I've played every single one since BFBC2 and BF1 and BFV top this game. I'd prefer it over every other title.   The only thing I really want from this game is proximity chat within 5-10m, specifically talking to vehicle crew not in your squad. Gunplay is A1. Visuals are A1, if your PC can handle it. Maps are a 7/10 (for me 9/10, but I'm trying to be reasonable), although, personally, I can enjoy every map given the teams are balanced and you don't get cornered. The maps will feel like hell for people who like to run and gun and not take it slow sometimes (in open areas). Most of the complaints I see are with performance, gameplay tactics that are unpopular (e.g. mortar/AA in team territory/strength of aircraft), and with the speed of updates.   Regarding crashing, I think the game is stable, but PC owners can have so many different configs not to mention people messing with their own OS so take it with a grain of salt. My specs are attached and I play on high w/ some settings maxed above 100fps, no issues.  Almost every gameplay tactic 'issue' has a counter.. everyone is just too lazy to be the one to do it so they complain. Mortar in team territory can be solved with counter mortar. Non-Issue, and yes I'm the counter mortar. Oppressive Aircraft just needs AA on the ground (which no one likes to carry) to make them pop flares and a decent attack chopper pilot to finish them off with AA missile (aka team effort), or AA on ground to force pilots to fly low which renders them open to small arms/tanks/rpgs. AA in spawn... who cares? if you're the enemy, they literally leave capture points open to attack from the air, and if that's your teammate, they prevent spawn camping from enemy aircraft and provide a retreat area for friendly aircraft. Moot argument imo. As far as updates... its EA and none of you can develop games so there's two reasons right there you should just sit and wait. Back in my day we didn't even get updates and no info regarding what to look forward to was given LOL. Seriously though, I rather them take their time and make a sound update than release hot garbage. I think BF6 has another year of updates in it. Go play another game if you cant wait, so many out there!  Battle Royal is fun if you can communicate. People complain about grinding attachments and tbh, its not a big deal. you can gain exp in bot lobbies if you want for weapons and vehicles. People just want a game handed to them, but BF has never been a franchise to do that, plus working to max a weapons actually makes you feel a sense of accomplishment... like a game should. This isn't a difficult game for me I might add, so this opinion could be bias. some of the challenges can be hard in pvp if you cant kill other players. They brought back squad upgrades and squad lead obj marking (peak). Every class is useful, but your lobbies will be 70% support and engineers. A good squad that talks and works together can no-diff the entire lobby. Campaign is just training.  Game is a solid 8/10 in my book. 9/10 with friends.

30 minutes in...  can't stop watching.  This 'game' is weird.  So far, it's mostly been what seems to be introductions, characters in the game introducing themselves to each other.   They're speaking Korean, I believe, though it sounds like Japanese, but I'm seeing their conversation typed out, translated to English.    And it's frickin' hilarious.  You ever find something that's strange, a bit off, bizarrely specific, and you find yourself sucked in, can't stop watching, cuz the underlying story is good?  The writing is very evocative, visceral, detailed, like describing a shimmer vibrating through your soul.  But, it's like the translation to English is off, just enough, so it's even more surreal, which elevates it a level.  Past 30 minutes, been staring at these people talking to each other, rapt, hand pressed to my forehead, saying out loud "WTF?" and then busting up laughing, over and over.  "I heard you glued our bodies back together from pieces.  I look forward to working with you."   "She gave a polite bow before returning to her seat."

Stop releasing new content when the same glitches from launch are still present. We don't want new things to spend money on, we want a game that works! We want a game that we paid full price for to function and not push paid content on us! I used to play the game for 8-10 hours straight with only bathroom breaks and short food breaks between with friends, when I first got it after the Sony account fiasco. We can't stand to play more than one operation maybe once a week now. The enemies clip through the environment and still damage you while doing so. Arrowhead nerfs weapons for "balance" only to buff them or "fix" them again when they release a warbond centered on it. The new warbond is basically just cobbled together, titled for the mechs that only take up about 1/4 of the warbond. The armor passive for the warbond? Move faster (outside of a mech). What does that have to do with exo-suits?  Every update more bugs and glitches crop up, that the devs refuse to fix because it's being "downprioritized" in favor of new content, Nice attempt at damage control, Arrowhead, but we didn't get a single good answer from you.  Arrowhead will claim to listen to the community, but they take genuine criticism (though it can be harsh) as hate and vitriol. They only listen to the discord, which is carefully curated by mods to have members that mostly just hype them up. They will not listen to the broader community unless we hit them where it counts; their wallet.  I'm so sad and disappointed by the game. It was so fun, it had so much potential, but I don't think it can survive Arrowhead and Sony now. Unless a lot of things change really fast.

With over 400hrs in this game and having been playing since the very begining including every major event, I can confidentaly say that the direction this game is going is going to make this game a insuffurable live service game. Why in god's name would one continue to play it then? This game was fun. Good combat and team mechanics. But with time, the game has significantly deteriorated in quality. Current (April 2026) complaints: 1. Lack of meaningful customisation to equipment, strategems and destroyer that provide true variety to gameplay. 2. Balancing is at a critical point. The balance of our impact on enemies vs enemies' impact on players is severely skewed. Ragdoll issues, getting overwhelmingly swarmed with extremely slim chances of success. This makes the game frustrating, with no incentive for improving player skill/strategic thinking. Even the friendly NPCs (SEAF soldiers - regular military) have more health points compared to the player who is supposed to be a member of the elite force. 3. Bug/balancing fixes are tied to paid warbonds. The devs could easily fix at least 50% of the issues players complain about (e.g. fire effect on enemies, suppressor use, mech units being weak) but choose to not fix it until a relevant paid warbond gets released (e.g. Stealth playstyle/supporessore - Redacted Regiment; Fire damage/effect - Entrenched Division; Mech vulnerability - Exo Experts) 4. Unbalanced galactic war. This issue is 2-fold. No meaningful way to encourage players to participate in Major Orders/reward participation/discourage non-participation. This is worse when new warbonds (which usually come with big plot critical Major Orders drop - see Cyberstan offensive) because you get a bunch of players playing non-MO worlds/missions to farm Super Credits. Additionally, the poor balancing of especially new enemies makes many players play non-MO planets, further hindering galactic war progression. 5. No meaningful progression once you hit approx Level 80-100. Yes, there are new weapons to grind for and super credits to earn for new warbonds. But none of them feel like they make a difference. The new warbond becomes meta, then it gets nerfed soon after, requiring you to farm for the next warbond. This is a result of the lack of customisation, and lack of balancing mentiond earlier. 6. Extremely poor dev-player engagement. The approach taken by community managers/Arrowhead/Discord Mods/player base has led to some regretable events (see Dev D10 challennge). Devs are very quick to push patches when a weapon becomes meta/players enjoy a playstyle. But drag their feet when it's something that they need to fix/buff (see fleshmob cliipping bug, previous lackluster fire effect, vehicle vulnerability damage, insane ragdoll effect, reduced player health). The recent QnA by Pilestedt was extremely tone-deaf. 7. Recent poor game design. Instead of creating legitimate challenges to challenge the player base and encourage better play, the devs prefer to just nerf the player while at the same time, introducing annoying enemies and mechanics for which players have very little available to address appropriately. This is not about the player not getting a power fantasy, this is about frustrating the players with an issue that has no answer available.

I bought this game a little late after the release, which I kind of regret, mainly because there was not that number of "balance" patches, just a lot of different tools and weapons to use, in its own meta, as I perceive it not in a greedy capitalist sense. The game is still fun, alone, with random people and [u]specially[/u] with friends, this game still has a lot to offer, but beware:   - 1. You get new weapons and tools by buying Warbonds, which you can achieve by farming in the game (which is boring if you aim to a full stack of 1000 Super Credits: it's lowest price for many Warbonds or you can pay 10€ for it). I just got tired of paying those 10€ over and over (to use the content on time before they "balance" it) to keep paying them and get not much in return (I expected free content for everyone since I support economically further the game). Let's say that lately, as a "coincidence" they patch some assets (buffing or balance) to the content they're gonna sell next, in the new Warbonds, past history in that regard doesn't speak in favour of Arrowhead, and the community has always been clear about this facts.  1.1. To actually use the content inside those Warbonds you'll need another currency that are called Medals, which you get from Major Orders and successfully completing missions. Recently this has been changed and Arrowhead is trying to force logs into the game (it's good to show login numbers I guess) to collect rewards of completed Major Orders, before this you could log in after months and get all the previous and current rewards for those.  1.2. There are 10 difficulties; playing in the hardest will get you more Samples (another currency that you need extracting alive to progress, eventually useless) but it won't get you more Super Credits (not more quantity when it spawns and not as a higher number of spawns), fairly enough they give you more currency out of hardest missions so you can invest it on upgrading weapons and buying other upgrades and stratagems (eventually it will be the only progression to invest in weapons besides Warbonds).  - 2. The Lore at the start was great, I stopped getting hyped for it because now for me it feels empty and/or counterintuitive, at first we got some rewards out of Major Orders (besides medals), we could even choose the strategic approaches and even it's rewards, we had options, now it feels like throwing rotten carrots to starving mules.  2.1. When it comes to complete Major Orders you don't actually get accurate information as a player (inside the game, so you rely on external info) to make wise choices as where, when, why and how to handle planetary threats, not to mention the number of missions that are completed for a Major Order, without taking into account the difficulty of those that are completed. I think Helldivers could have way better tools to coordinate/communicate about Major Orders inside the game, rather than relying on Reddit, Discord or other third-party tools.  - 3. The stability of the game has been almost always like a rollercoaster, crashes, freezes, tons of bugs, many of them ruining an entire mission, unfair when it comes to enemy factions (specially new missions new enemies, etc.), Arrowhead has been trying to fix this issues for quite some time, or that's what I believe, until they stopped or in some cases they made it even worse, and yes, this is still a thing, you can still encounter many unfair and silly bugs today.  Some of this mistakes are supposed to be a permanent, purposefully made changes, besides the clear communication the community had about it.  3.1. Patchlogs usually are precise, until the community started to express it's opinions on some changes, then some people made the decision to not mention some important changes, which you had to rely on the Helldivers wiki to have some real transparency about the changes made.  Same goes for enemy information, such as weakpoints, strategies... There's nothing like a catalogue in the game, which I guess would make sense for the Ministry of Intelligence, once again you rely on external sources.  - 4. Progression, this one is tough to swallow, besides getting all the new content behind paywalled Warbonds (offering less and less content over time), your progression will stop once you reach around level 50 (the maximum level being 150) you will just gather experience for the sake of it, nothing meaningful. No endgame content related to that, nothing at all.  I tried to sum everything here, so you know where are you going and what has been the experience of older players, don't get me wrong, I've enjoyed a LOT this game, weirdly enough; after the PlayStation login issue we achieved a great communication studio-community wise. I just don't feel like I should support this kind of decisions anymore, even if it's out of nostalgia, I strongly recommend this game with some friends, try everything out, coordinate and have tons of fun, I'll personally wait until I see some changes about everything I mentioned.

Elden Ring is an immersive game with almost an infinite number of ways to play the game and build ideas to explore. It is one of the most challenging yet rewarding games I have played. Elden Ring is my favorite game to the souls series of games to date. I have not had this much fun with a game in a long time.

One Ring made by the evil lord Sauron Hidetaka Miyazaki, One Ring to rule them all, One Ring to find them, One Ring to bring them all and in the darkness bind them, In the Land of Japanese software development warehouse building where Shadows lie.  Elden Ring is not worth your time due to the profound curse placed upon the game's core design by the evil man who made it. The game aims at being infinitely replayable, but each of its assets is designed in a narrow fashion - so much so that the world does not feel as open as it is rather episodic. Each and every instance in the game is instrumented to be seen and encountered in a specific way which makes the experience of world exploration predictable and less exciting after playing for the first time. The dificulty ranges of the various explorable regions of the game world are awfully imballanced, so much so that it's less enjoyable to avoid choosing a linear path through the game on later playthroughs. The game world's incredibly size makes this issue even more frustrating - in order to experience something new on a subsequent playthrough, the player has to cross massive distances which are increasingly less and less aproachable the further he is from the starting point, ultimately discouraging exploration and narrowing the game experience to a single, most optimalized path of progression. There are only so many ways to reexperience the game's world without being severely discouraged and punished. And so ultimately, the game world's size and the quantity of its assets is severely nullified as the various paths to approach them are tragically so very limited.  The game's world becomes eventually too predictable and the premise of experiencing the game in a fresh way nonexistent, which is not fun. Let it be pronounced and heard, Elden Ring could be legendary, but it is not, and won't be, unless FromSoftware developpers cease to practice the habit of forgetting a game once it's been created, despite the quantity of bothersome features still present and still widely acknowledged. One may recall the pathos of never properly finishing Lost Izalith in their previous game Dark Souls, an area of the game so very unique and all the more so disappointing for being critically underdevelopped. I feel very simillar about Elden Ring.  As a former world of warcraft player, I see fit to pronounce a variety of ideas which could severely improve the experience of Elden Ring if they were ever developped, as they are present in that particular game, and although a very different game at that, still possesing a massive world and the spirit of an rpg game.   Those ideas would be: There should be a variety of start points in Elden Ring, or at the very least, it should be possible to cross into other regions of the game from Limgrave without being stupefied and severely punished as much as is the case now. There should be more low level regions surrounding Limgrave so as to increase replayability and the possibilities of exploration on future playthroughs. And yes, despite the existence of various teleportation chests in Limgrave, the fact that the places you are sent to are completely above Limgrave's difficulty makes them less relevant as a travel feature. There is not enough enemy variety at each of the game's various places - an occasional "rare enemy spawn" or the promise of a "rare item drop" would be a solution to this issue. And lastly, there should be more paths connecting the various regions and places of the Elden Ring world and more accessible ways to connect the game's many features.  In conclusion, Elden Ring nowhere near as fun as enjoyable after the first couple of playthroughs, it becomes stale and predictable rather too fast, and that is a completely nonsensical feature for a game of such high ambition and such quantity of the game's features.

Red Dead Redemption 2 delivers one of the most powerful and emotionally layered stories ever told in a video game. Set against the fading days of the American frontier, the narrative follows Arthur Morgan, an outlaw struggling to reconcile loyalty to his gang with his own evolving sense of morality. What makes the story so compelling isn’t just the overarching plot, but the depth of its characters and the way their relationships shift over time.  Arthur’s journey is the heart of the experience. He begins as a hardened enforcer, but as the story unfolds, players witness a gradual transformation shaped by hardship, loss, and reflection. This character development feels natural and earned, largely because the writing gives space for quiet, introspective moments alongside the more dramatic events. Conversations around campfires, journal entries, and small acts of kindness all contribute to a sense that Arthur is a real person rather than just a typical protagonist.  The supporting cast is equally strong. Each member of the gang has distinct motivations and personalities, and the tension within the group grows in a believable way. Dutch, the gang’s charismatic leader, is particularly well-written—his idealism slowly unravels, creating a sense of unease that builds steadily throughout the game. These interpersonal conflicts elevate the story beyond a simple tale of outlaws, turning it into a study of loyalty, trust, and the consequences of blind faith.  Another strength of the story is its pacing and tone. It takes its time, allowing players to fully immerse themselves in the world and its characters. While some may find the slower pace challenging, it ultimately pays off by making the emotional moments hit much harder. By the time the story reaches its climax, there’s a genuine sense of attachment and loss that few games manage to achieve.  Overall, the storytelling in Red Dead Redemption 2 stands out for its maturity, attention to detail, and emotional impact. It’s not just a story about the Wild West—it’s a deeply human tale about change, regret, and the search for redemption.

I have 400hrs on this account and another 5-600 (at least) on my PS4. I have close to 10k matches played across all platforms at this point. I've played Third Strike, SF4, and SF5. After all this time spent on this game, and on fighters generally, I can say that I don't think this game is very good.   I keep waiting for it to improve and to be enjoyable to play like Third Strike and 4, but I don't think this game will ever be anywhere near as good/fun to play as those. I'd even settle for it being as good as 5, but after three years in I just don't think that is going to happen. This isn't me saying that those games were perfect, they all had their issues for sure. However, in spite of those issues those games were always "fun" to play. I wonder how much of that fun was due to the fact that not only were there more opportunities to play people in real life, but that the optimal way to play those games period was against other people in real life.   For the first few hundred matches this will feel fresh and exciting due the accelerated pacing the game gets from the drive system. Unfortunately, this really homogenizes the gameplay and messes up the character balance in a way I'm not sure is fixable. Some characters can glide across the screen instantly, some characters barely go a quarter of the way, and some characters have a DR that move at a snail's pace. Even if a character has a bad DR it doesn't change much of their game plan, which for all characters is essentially: "connect with your longest range DR cancelable poke and let it rip into a plus button for strike/throw/shimmy mix".   The game is boring to play, boring to watch, and frustrating in a way that the games in the past were not. Low forward into drive rush punishes any attempt to reposition, even a little bit. The pressure that players get even off a blocked low forward into drive rush is too much. Corner carry and corner pressure are insane in this game. Some characters (Ken, Sagat, etc.) can take you to the corner from mid screen in one combo and once you are in the corner you're stuck in a blender of throw loop/shimmy pressure.  All that having been said, I do think it's a game where the better player wins. The problem isn't with fairness or balance or "scrubbing people out" like salt lords might say. The problem is that the game just isn't fun. If there were any other fighter with as much money put into its development, play testing, and advertising/marketing people would be playing that. We are in a fighting game drought and we are going to be there until they rework this game to be enjoyable for more than 20 hours or until we get something that's actually fun to play for a long period of time. Until then we have this game, Tekken 8, and MK1. Yikes.   At any rate, if you're enjoying the game I'm happy for you and genuinely hope you continue to enjoy it. Maybe I'm just old and jaded and worn out on video games in general. Maybe I'm too nostalgic for things that weren't as good as I'm remembering them. Maybe season four will give us some substantial balance changes that will make this game enjoyable for me. Until I figure out what's wrong with me, or Capcom figures out what's wrong with the game, I don't think there's anything for me in SF6.

I honestly don't think I can recommend this, at least not to players that have the same mindset that I do when it comes to playing a game that you invest this much time into only to be blue balled with the ending.  That being said, I just finished my 2nd playthrough (first one somehow had me finishing the game and giving me achievements even though I couldn't get through Adam Smasher. So I technically only have 1 complete play through) and I was severely underwhelmed by the entire experience.  It felt like your entire journey as V was cheapened into some pre-determined outcome and you were just along for the ride.  I felt like I didn't really have a satisfying outcome with The Sun.  It didn't make any sense.  Didn't give you any closure. Everything went to hell and back and left you feeling just hollow and numb to it all. And then the build up (if you can even call it that) was so flat and ill prepared that it just made the ending make no sense at all.  maybe it was just that ending, but I clocked that from the moment you accept the Hanako Embers quest until you can get through all the "ending extras"?  You're at just under 2 hours of actual time between points.  I'm not wanting to spend 2 hours in the hopes that I 'get a better ending, because this one sucked royally'.  That's not how I really operate.  So if you're like me and you're hoping for "yeah. Let's go have a satisfying ending"?  You don't have high chances of that happening, sadly.  Prepare yourself for it, because the game will let you down if you at least end up with The Sun for an ending.  I enjoyed going through everything and I would also recommend to finish up everything in Phantom Liberty before going to the Embers quest: because you "can't go back afterwards", you can just simply "repeat and try again".  Like a slot machine.  Also game needs more Panam.    8/10 overall but 5/10 for the lame ending I got.    Took the wind right out of my sails and I have no desire to go back any time soon to "try again".

I put off on buying this game for the longest time after hearing about how broken and under-baked it was on launch. And boy, it must have been something atrocious, because even now after what I can only assume has been dozens of major fixes and additions, it is still a buggy mess. Within the first 30 minutes of the game, I managed to get myself stuck in the player stash room because for some reason if you close the door after entering the room there is no way to open the door again. After leaving the apartment, a woman sitting with her back to the balcony stood up, warped upwards onto the railing, turned around and walked off and plumeted 60 feet to her death. I would often see, in one area, 4-5 NPCs with the exact same model only to turn around 180 degrees and see another group all sporting a different identical model. It was so bad, in fact, I was impressed if I was able to look somewhere other than the sky and NOT see at least two identical models in my field of view. I couldn't walk 20 feet without seeing broken animations. A plaza with 5 people all sitting down with their arms raised above their heads like they're being held at gunpoint, but conversing with each other completely normally. I can't count on the fingers I have how many people were managing to smoke with the cigarette only ever coming within 6 inches of their mouth.  Maybe the rest of the story is good. Maybe the gameplay is good. But I wouldn't know, because 4 hours into the game I am still in what appears to be the most boring and context deprived prologue I've ever experienced, and I can't even save myself by just deciding to free roam because you're restricted to a roughly 2 square foot area of the city during this time. If you decide to venture outside that area, too bad, you get a message on the screen that literally says "there's nothing out there for you", your screen goes black, and your car magically flips around.  Well, there's nothing in here for me either.

[h2] If you're reading this review and you're contemplating on whether or not to buy this game, let me make the decision for you. BUY THIS GAME! [/h2]  I cannot even begin to put into words how amazing and fun this game is. Terraria is a one-of-a-kind game- There's so much to do, and you can pour hundreds of hours of playtime into this game without even realizing it. I have 425 or so hours on Terraria, split between four playthroughs and I'm still not done playing. I still have so many things I want to do on this game, which is why you should play it. I'll give you a few reasons too!  To start, Terraria has so much in-game content it's honestly difficult to NOT find something to do on this game. This game is far from "2D Minecraft" as it's often described. On the surface it might seem so, but when you start playing it becomes harder and harder to view it as just a 2D survival. There are so many things to collect, it's actually hilarious. Imagine anything you want; you will find it in this game. There are guns, bows, whips, launchers, and that's just scratching the surface. You can literally command a little army of minions to fight for you. Even then--- the minions you can find in this game are absurd! You can have mini UFOs to fight for you. While on the topic of weapons, let me talk about CLASSES.  Now yes, this game has specific classes you can play as. As if it couldn't get any better! There are four main damage types in the game, melee, ranged, magic, and summon. There's epic swords, guns made from sharks, crazy magic wands and even magic GUNS. These all come with corresponding armor sets and accessories in order to be able to fight at your strongest. Not to mention that they're also pretty well balanced-- in my opinion. From the start of the game until the finish, you will ALWAYS, and I mean ALWAYS find some new bazonkers weapon that will (sometimes) be able to crush ANYTHING in your path. It's wild!  But let me mention the difficulty of the game. This game is NOT EASY!! (Sometimes). At some points it very much will be, but you will DEFINITELY face threats in this game that are sometimes hard enough that it will make you reconsider if you'll be able to even reach the final boss, ESPECIALLY when it comes to Master Mode. Every boss will offer something different, and you'll have to adapt to anything and everything new you face. I swear I've developed observation haki from playing through Master Mode THREE TIMES. Sometimes the bosses will be more relaxed; not much will happen during the fight, or it will be an absolute bullet hell that will shred you to bits. There is a VERY BIG learning curve in this game, too. You WILL struggle, but that's something that I think makes the game fun, because you're driven to explore and find other weapons to workaround the weaknesses of others.  And even when you think you've gone through everything you possibly could in the vanilla game, this game has MOD SUPPORT. The amount of mods you can find in this game is likely endless, though I haven't played through any yet myself. But there are some awesome looking mods that I've seen that will make me sink even more hours into this game.  If you buy this game, you will have zero regrets. After over 600 hours of survival, I can confidently say that Project Zomboid isn't just a game; it's the most honest 'how you died' simulator ever made. I have followed Build 42 from its early experimental stages (B42.41) to the current iteration (B42.17.0), and the evolution has been nothing short of extraordinary.  Positive Highlights: -Unmatched System Depth: The overhaul of the lighting propagation and depth buffer in Build 42 has created a far more haunting and immersive atmosphere than ever before. -Realistic Crafting: The expansion of the tech tree—including blacksmithing, pottery, and brewing—provides satisfying long-term goals for players looking to rebuild civilization from the ground up. -A Living World: The addition of livestock and wildlife has transformed survival from simple looting to sustainable resource management.   Constructive Feedback: -UI Learning Curve: Some of the newer crafting UI elements feel a bit complex for long-time players. Refining these to be more intuitive would greatly improve the gameplay flow. -Mid-Game Optimization: With the influx of new features in Build 42, performance while facing large hordes in dense urban areas still needs some polish to remain stable on mid-range hardware.

I am happy to see the progress the devs are making, build 42 sparked my interest again. The learning curve is perfect and I enjoy the difficulty in this game. I also really enjoy the lore and how you can find out so much just by playing the game and looking for clues. The week one mod is also really fun and I wonder if there could be a preset mode that's like the mod. I feel its perfect for immersion and setting up who your character really was and who they died as.

Rimworld is an amazing game, well worth the price. You start small, crash landing on a planet with barely anything, or nothing at all depending on the starting scenario and then harvest, build, trade and research to improve things for your growing colony. Every day you're dealing with new threats: raids, blights, injuries, infections, toxic fallout, insect hives, forest fires and there's always the looming threat that haunts the nightmares of your colonists... "Ate without table -3"  It's a great game that has only improved with each careful patch, and there's a veritable host of high-quality mods to adjust things to your liking once you are familiar with how everything works.  The DLCs are not essential to enjoyment but each new DLC refreshes the game, adding to and changing the experience in welcome ways and enticing you back to start a new colony or two. The free patch alongside each DLC can feel equally impactful, with lots of quality of life changes and improvements. I await each new DLC eagerly and have so far always been pleasantly surprised even by DLC that I didn't expect to like (Anomaly).  A summary of each DLC: Odyssey: Adds ships and transports, orbital mechanics and new orbital traders, unique weapons, animals and biomes. Rewards you with more ways to explore the world while also making exploring the world more rewarding. The ships are a little easier to get than I'd like while the unique weapons and items added are a little rarer than I'd like (I'd consider adding some to orbital traders), but I understand why. The ships are easy to get you exploring and the items are so rare to make special locations feel worthwhile to explore. But I personally love that the exotic orbital trader (with Biotech) now can sell polux seeds and wish I could find more of the unique stuff trading.  Anomaly: Adds horror elements, more quests and events (all interesting, some surprisingly difficult), many new things to capture and study. Rewards you with powerful rituals (and a new tree). Also the new music tracks are a fantastic addition to the background ambiance, for example: "Rimworld Anomaly OST #5 Abandoned By The Light - Alistair Lindsay"  Biotech: Adds genetics, mechs, children and pollution. New organic and mechanical enemies. Rewards you with helpful bots and the experience of raising children is one of the most impactful changes to the base game. Feels like 3 DLCs rolled into one. You can collect genes to print out super-soldiers in growth vats, or you can shun people and try to do everything with a growing army of robots, you can give birth or foster orphaned children and teach them new skills as they slowly hit key growth moments... or all of the above, raising an army of genetically engineered super-soldiers that each lead an army of giant killer robots, protecting your fortified nursery and classroom where your natural born colonists develop in peace and relative safety.  Ideology: Adds beliefs, roles, rituals. Helps to define each playthrough and adjust difficulty, you can be anything from transhumanists that seek to replace inferior flesh with arcotech, tree worshippers that have mental breaks when forced to chop wood to stay alive or even a cannibal cult that gets miserable eating anything but long pork. Also adds new trees that are underpowered compared to content that has been added since, but can be situationally useful.  Royalty: Adds the empire as a friend or foe. New advanced enemies like mech clusters and high tech items. New quests, new mechanics (honour, titles and psycasts - think magic spells). You can dip into royalty content with an ascetic colonist who doesn't care about all the thrones and titles just for a few psycasts and new trade options, or, you can go all-in making giant polished throne rooms as your colonists rise to the ranks of royalty and entertain the most honoured of guests.  Rimworld is great, and only getting better.

I was pretty excited to play this game, as i had always wanted better graphics for ark, Mainly due to my ♥♥♥♥♥♥ pc, this gamed lagged quite a bit. but ive seen smaller sized and way nicer games with better performance. when i finally launched the game. the graphics felt good. but there was still those stupid visual bugs the first game had. Which i had hoped this game fixed. either way i gave it a shot and played a couple hours with my friend. the game is Okay, but i personally don't recommend it.  Pros - Better Graphics(If your PC can handle it, unlike mine) - More features, such as a better Character Creation Screen(Which i do like), The ability to change a Structure's Form such as a wall to a doorway, and a mod selection screen, i'm not sure if this was in the first game as i played it on the ps4. a few DLCs i found, and the other maps are free to play. - A bigger overall map(The Island is the one i played for my 5.1 hours of gameplay) I do like how the map feels and looks overall bigger than that of the first games Island.  Cons - The old games visual bugs are still there, (like that one random line in the middle of the screen). -  Poor Performance even on the lowest settings, (Again, due to my ♥♥♥♥ PC Specs) -  Bugs, i found a few bugs in the game. (Those include Missing textures for the ground, rocks, dinosaurs and the map i couldn't even read or look at. A hair bug where my characters hair came off my characters head looking extremely ugly, As a person who loves creating my own characters in games, this felt really annoying) - The size of the game is huge, almost call of duty level, and ive seen better games with less storage taken and better graphics and performance.  My personal recommendation is getting this game around $10-$12 dollars USD when its on sale, if your pc can handle the improved graphics. but otherwise, just buy the first game ARK Survival Evolved.

ARK is still insanely (lovably) janky at its core (reviewing after plenty of hours on Xbox) but I do think this remaster works well for general improvements. Some maps got direct upgrades: The Island, Scorched Earth, (arguably) Extinction, but some got screwed.   Like, Aberration's fertile region looks waaaaaaay better, but the bioluminescent and element regions lost so much of what made them ominous, The half-scorched island on The Center is just completely forested now, and the volcano there is completely forested too (admittedly, that doesn't look too bad). You can't even enter Skull Island anymore!  Ragnarok's additions look tacked on for the most part, largely because their geometry feels at-odds with the rest of the map. The new islands lack beaches almost entirely, for some reason.  Yet still, I'm pretty content with all the upgrades here. I wouldn't dare say it's well optimized, but getting 120fps on Ultra graphics with my 5070ti is... something, I guess. Admittedly, the input latency is abysmal, but hopefully that'll get better with the Unreal update later this year...?

For reference: I hover between 5 and 6 star MMR and usually play solo  I used to really enjoy this game before the engine update, but not anymore. It just frustrates me now.  1) The bush-camper problem has not just stuck around, it's gotten way worse. Ever since the update almost every single game has one or multiple subsonic + silencer and/or hunting bow players, who don't do anything but sit in one spot for a significant part of the game and wait for an easy kill. This makes most games an absolute slog fest where I'm not actually FIGHTING any players, I'm just searching for them. It is not a fun experience.   2) Weapon and ammo balancing makes absolutely no sense right now.  Genuine question: Why would you ever pick a shotgun? A bow does everything a shotgun does, it's quiet, has a much further kill distance and is only 2 slots. Shotguns in general, but especially sawed-off ones have no reason to exist. Similar question: Why would I ever pick poison ammo on any gun? It got nerfed into the ground against PvE, which takes away it's main purpose and its completely useless against players most of the time because antidote is everywhere because of 4-shot boons. Just pick fire ammo instead, it's better than poison ammo at everything. Crytek clearly has a favourite child. And of course, the classic: Compact and medium ammo are still completely worthless compared to long ammo.  3) The devs have started making PvE significantly more frustrating, by increasing the range at which AI can spot you, making all AI follow you around for way longer, giving immolators the ability to set you on fire and introducing the firebreather. PvE was already easily the least interesting and least fun aspect of this game. Nobody asked for this. On top of that, I swear that AI pathfinding has become super inconsistent. I've had it happen several times where AI just runs right past me, runs away from me or shimmies to the side. I even had an armoured moonwalk away from me once. How and why exactly does this happen??  Dishonourable mention: Aim punch  The only reason I still play is because I hate other games more.

Hunt is unlike any game i have ever played in my life. I have had the fortune( and the misfortune) of playing various extraction shooters, but this one is an entire different league of its own. I got the game about a week ago and i already have close to 15 hours and i have a super busy work life so considering that, this game is doing bonkers!  This game pretty much nails every aspect that makes an extraction shooter a GOOD extraction shooter. The audio is mind-blowing, the ambience is gut-wrenching, the setting and the atmosphere down to every scene and every sound runs shivers down the spine. Add to that the crisp gun mechanics( and some pretty interesting guns), killing an enemy is about the most satisfying feeling I have had in the last 2 years of gaming. Seriously, the game developers need to be applauded for the map designs, the audio engineering and character/npc designs though the bosses, few in number, feel underwhelming at times. But it makes up for that with the grim temperament and the fact that you will literally hold your breath every damn time you get into a firefight with human enemy combatants.   There are so many variable factors and scenarios that just occur when you play this game. From hiding in a bush, witnessing your teammates getting pinned down by a trio, helpless to do anything, trying to ambush the enemy combatants, you try to crawl through the wheat fields, only to find that your path ahead is littered with grunts/zombies that will yell and charge you the second they see you. It adds thrill, annoyance, tension, nervousness and an innate, heavy need to THINK, to make DECISIONS, to CHOOSE a path that won't compromise you and your buddies, which is what makes an extraction shooter a GOOD EXTRACTION shooter. Thats what differentiates hunt from its other contemporaries. All of the above is further amplified with hunt's 3D audio design, that makes every waking moment in the game laced with a dreadful paranoia.  But hunt is not perfect. And often, the problem doesnt exactly lie in the game, but in the playerbase. The Asia servers are NOT good enough to provide an excellent gameplay experience. If you are from the indian subcontinent, you will face latency issues, packet loss issues and above all, the most annoying, language barrier issues. Majority of the players i have queued with, do not speak a single line of English, thus making it extremely difficult to coordinate with others. Even though coordination is the main ingredient and aspect of a game like hunt, you will be stripped bare of it if you play in the asian servers. The higher ping also majorly affects your gameplay, as hunt tends to be a slow, mostly one to two-shot game, so missing once or twice, which is highly likely due to your higher ping, will put you at great disadvantage. I also detest the bosses, i feel like they could provide a much more challenging obstacle rather than beiung runned down by almost every trio out there. Their character designs arent as special either, the butcher is a joke. They also should increase the arsenal and bring in some more variety and make the UI cleaner because its messy and is counter intuitive. Also add better tutorials tht also teach the importance of consumables and throwables. The current tutorial is lacklustre.  Apart from that, hunt is a massive game changer for the genre and the type of players it aims to please. Its definitely a recommend if you like challenging shooters and a HARD recommend if you like your shooters with an extra serving of retro, cowboy, existential horror. 8/10.

My Personal Review of World of Warships ⚓🌊  World of Warships is one of my favourite games… honestly, I really love it. I wanted to play together with my friend, so I even gifted them a Tier VIII battleship, hoping we could enjoy the game side by side. 😊  A long time ago, there used to be an independent Korean server for one of the Wargaming titles. In the case of World of Tanks, when the Korean server shut down and merged into the Asia server, I was so busy with my schedule that I completely forgot about the transfer period. I also don’t remember receiving any clear refund notice at the time.  Then, my account disappeared… and all the premium tanks I had bought with real money were just gone. I tried to find the account later, but I couldn’t recover it. Since then, I honestly lost all interest in the tank game. I don’t even watch it anymore… 😢  For World of Warships, I originally played on the Asia server. I remember seeing the Korean server close and merge with the Asia server too, and honestly, I wasn’t exactly thrilled about it. At some point, the Asia server seemed to support Korean, which was convenient, and I did appreciate that.  Still, I wanted to play on a foreign server, so I started again on the European server through Steam. Steam was very convenient for purchases, so I bought a Tier VIII battleship that is no longer sold now. I also worked really hard on the Soviet cruiser line. Around the time when Moskva was being removed or changed, I pushed all the way up to Tier X because the Soviet cruiser tree was my main focus.  My main account is still on the Asia server through the official client, and I still enjoy playing there. But when Steam linking became available, I accidentally linked my main account. Then I remembered my European server account, cancelled it, and tried to link Steam again so I could keep playing on the EU server.  But somehow, things became complicated. I couldn’t smoothly access the European server anymore, and when I finally managed to get in, my ships seemed to be reset or missing. I couldn’t enjoy the game properly after that. Honestly… why was it designed to be so complicated? 😭  So whenever I see World of Warships on Steam, I feel a little sad. Using Steam for payments is genuinely convenient, and I really love battleship games. These days, there seem to be more female players too, which makes the game feel even more fun and welcoming. But not being able to play properly through Steam makes me feel really disappointed.  That’s why I don’t play this game through Steam anymore. I just use the official client. Of course, the friend I brought into the game plays through Steam, so I feel a tiny bit jealous… hehe. 😅  I just wanted to write down a bit of my personal experience. Even with all of that, I still think World of Warships is an absolutely brilliant game. I would definitely recommend it. It’s fun for both men and women, and anyone can enjoy it.  The visual effects have improved so much recently, and when you look out at the ocean, the sunset, and the sky, it actually feels calming. Sometimes it almost feels like therapy… haha. 🌅🌊  But don’t forget — if you mess around too much in battle, you’ll become a burden to your team and might cause everyone to lose. So please play properly! 😆  Still… sometimes, when I think about playing on the European server, I really miss those friends I met there. 💙

This is the worst game I’ve ever played. After 400+ hours, every single premium ship I own—Shimakaze, iwami, Shoukaku, even the brand-new Aberdeen—loses money, every single battle.  I lose credits whether I win or lose. I lose credits even when I top damage and carry the team. Even with a guild economic bonus, I still walk away minus 7,000 credits.  The only consistent outcome of playing this game is disgust and rage. It feels designed not to be enjoyed—but to drain your wallet while making you feel worthless.  Play like a normal player? You get one-shot by predicted torpedoes before leaving spawn. Or you’re magically pushed out of cover and evaporated in seconds by “core hits” that defy armor mechanics. I wouldn’t be surprised if there are unchecked cheats—the matchmaking feels that broken.  In Co-op, teammates steal all your kills. In Randoms, you face overpowered opponents with perfect aim and radar spam. Even in PvE, enemy AI dodges Shimakaze’s torpedoes better than human players—ships instantly stop or turn on a dime like they’re controlled by gods.  I’m done. This beast of a game deserves to shut down. Don’t waste your time. Don’t waste your money. Just leave.

Honestly a really solid game that I WANT to recommend, but it just feels unpolished and disjointed.  The Combat feels awkward since your character deals near zero damage, but you can't just sick a pal on the boss and watch, since occasionally the boss with throw an attack at your character and you'll die in one or two hits.    The base building feels like its been completely forgotten, since there is little to no actual automation: pals can do the work, and faster than you but still VERY slowly, and only if you manually tell them to.  You can't just say stand here and smelt ingots and have them go grab more ore if its available when they run out, you have to manually return to base, go to the furnace and tell them to smelt however many more you have the ore for.  And resources are PAINFULLY scarce compared to the amount that is consumed.  Trying to break through to better automation is near impossible, since you need literally hundreds of ingots for a single building, which translates to dozens of trips to ore veins, not counting any ore needed to repair your tools or armor.    All in all it feels like someone looked at Conan Exiles, said "hey what if we strapped a summoning system on it" and then forgot to actually mix all the elements correctly.  Really hoping that after all the lawsuit ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ is wrapped up they can stop working on collabs long enough to actually finish the game.

Buy this game if you have anxiety about driving a car for the first time. Buy a wheel and pedals. Crash a few cars with ai traffic. Get over your fears. Then get your liscence. Thats what this game did for me. I was terrified to drive a car. I didnt want to die young bc of a mistake i made behind the wheel. So i learned the rules of the road on beam ng. No sim seat. Just a wheel and pedals. Ive been driving for 2 years now and i can say that if it werent for beamng drive id still be too scared to sit in a driver seat.
If you're coming from Forza Horizon or some other open world driving game, BeamNG.drive takes it to another level with realistic car functions and insane physics.

If you told me I would be obsessing over this game before I bought it, I wouldn't have believed you. I was bullied into buying this game to play with my partner because it was given good reviews as a co-op. This game has robot whimsy like Portal, fun exploration like Minecraft, and the intense planning and execution of Stardew Valley. All wrapped into one game that won't stress you out because you died, but because your factory shut down for the upteenth time and you can't figure out why. It's not a game, it's your new lifestyle. 10/10 would recommend.
